content.text: Handling file uploads and form submissions requires a fundamentally different parsing model than JSON APIs — multipart boundaries, streaming reads, and memory management become first-class concerns the moment binary content enters the picture.
File uploads and form data in FastAPI cover two related but distinct HTTP content types:
— the default HTML form encoding, where field names and values are percent-encoded in the request body as
pairs. Used for simple text field submissions.
— a boundary-delimited encoding that supports both text fields and binary file content in the same request body. Required whenever file uploads are involved.
FastAPI exposes these through three primary abstractions:
for file content as raw bytes, and
for streaming file access with metadata. All three are built on Starlette's multipart parsing infrastructure, which in turn uses the

REST APIs that only consume JSON cover most use cases — but the moment an application needs profile photos, document imports, CSV data ingestion, audio/video uploads, or any binary content, a different content model is required. Browsers cannot submit files as JSON natively. Native mobile apps, desktop clients, and data pipelines all rely on multipart form submissions for binary transfer.
Getting file handling wrong has direct operational consequences:
Loading large files entirely into memory causes OOM crashes under concurrent load
Missing content type validation allows malicious file uploads
Not streaming uploads blocks other requests on the event loop
Naively trusting client-supplied filenames enables path traversal attacks
usage — FastAPI does not bundle it:
at startup when form-related routes are detected.
signals to FastAPI that this parameter comes from the form body, not a query string or JSON payload.

from typing import Optional

@app.post("/profile/")
async def update_profile(
    display_name: str = Form(...),
    bio: Optional[str] = Form(default=None),
    website: str = Form(default=""),
    is_public: bool = Form(default=True)
):
    return {
        "display_name": display_name,
        "bio": bio,
        "website": website,
        "is_public": is_public
    }
FastAPI coerces form field strings to the declared types.
function accepts the same validators as
FastAPI offers two ways to receive uploaded files:
Entire file loaded into memory immediately
Spooled to disk; streaming read available
under the hood — content below a size threshold is kept in memory, and content above it is spilled to disk automatically.

from fastapi import UploadFile, File

@app.post("/upload/")
async def upload_file(file: UploadFile = File(...)):
    # Metadata
    print(file.filename)       # Original filename from client: "report.pdf"
    print(file.content_type)   # MIME type from Content-Type header: "application/pdf"
    print(file.size)           # File size in bytes (available after reading on some versions)
    print(file.headers)        # All part headers from the multipart form

    # Reading
    all_bytes = await file.read()         # Read entire file → bytes
    chunk = await file.read(8192)         # Read up to 8192 bytes
    await file.seek(0)                    # Reset to beginning
    first_bytes = await file.read(16)     # Read again from start

    # The underlying file-like object
    raw = file.file                       # SpooledTemporaryFile — use for libraries expecting file objects

    return {
        "filename": file.filename,
        "content_type": file.content_type,
        "size": len(all_bytes)
    }
between multiple reads of the same file — reads are positional and do not reset automatically.
parts in the multipart body. From HTML:
Form fields and file uploads can be mixed in the same multipart request — this is one of the primary advantages of

from fastapi import Form, File, UploadFile
from typing import Optional

@app.post("/articles/")
async def create_article(
    title: str = Form(...),
    body: str = Form(...),
    category: str = Form(...),
    tags: str = Form(default=""),             # Comma-separated tags as a string
    cover_image: Optional[UploadFile] = File(default=None),
    attachments: List[UploadFile] = File(default=[])
):
    tag_list = [t.strip() for t in tags.split(",") if t.strip()]

    article = await save_article(title=title, body=body, category=category, tags=tag_list)

    if cover_image:
        image_url = await store_file(cover_image)
        await update_article_image(article.id, image_url)

    attachment_urls = []
    for attachment in attachments:
        url = await store_file(attachment)
        attachment_urls.append(url)

    return {
        "article_id": article.id,
        "title": title,
        "cover_image_url": image_url if cover_image else None,
        "attachments": attachment_urls
    }
(JSON) cannot coexist in the same endpoint. A request body is either
— not both. If JSON data needs to accompany a file, serialize it as a string in a form field and parse it manually, or use a separate metadata endpoint before the upload.
Reading the entire file into memory with
is dangerous for large files under concurrent load. Stream the content in chunks instead:
for the disk write ensures the write operation is non-blocking — it runs in a thread pool, keeping the event loop free for other requests.
header of the multipart part — it is supplied by the client and cannot be trusted alone:
File signatures (magic bytes) are the reliable way to determine actual file content:
Size can be checked before reading via the
header (when available) or enforced during streaming:

@app.post("/upload/")
async def upload_with_size_check(
    request: Request,
    file: UploadFile = File(...)
):
    # Check Content-Length header (not always present or trustworthy)
    content_length = request.headers.get("content-length")
    max_size = 10 * 1024 * 1024  # 10MB

    if content_length and int(content_length) > max_size:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=413, detail="File too large")

    # Enforce during read (reliable)
    content = b""
    async for chunk in file:
        content += chunk
        if len(content) > max_size:
            raise HTTPException(status_code=413, detail="File exceeds 10MB limit")

    return {"size": len(content)}
Never trust the client-supplied filename for filesystem operations:

import os
import re
from pathlib import Path

def sanitize_filename(filename: str) -> str:
    """
    Produce a safe filename for filesystem storage.
    - Strips directory components (path traversal protection)
    - Removes or replaces dangerous characters
    - Limits length
    """
    if not filename:
        return "unnamed_file"

    # Extract just the filename, discard any path components
    name = Path(filename).name

    # Remove null bytes and control characters
    name = re.sub(r'[\x00-\x1f\x7f]', '', name)

    # Replace spaces and dangerous characters with underscores
    name = re.sub(r'[<>:"/\\|?*\s]', '_', name)

    # Collapse multiple underscores
    name = re.sub(r'_+', '_', name).strip('_')

    # Limit total length (preserving extension)
    stem = Path(name).stem[:100]
    suffix = Path(name).suffix[:10]
    name = stem + suffix

    return name or "unnamed_file"

# Usage
safe_name = sanitize_filename("../../etc/passwd")        # → "etc_passwd" (no path traversal)
safe_name = sanitize_filename("my report (final).pdf")   # → "my_report_final.pdf"
safe_name = sanitize_filename("image.jpg")               # → "image.jpg"
For stored files, prefer using a generated UUID as the actual filename and storing the original name only in the database:

import uuid

async def store_upload(file: UploadFile) -> dict:
    storage_key = f"{uuid.uuid4()}{Path(file.filename).suffix.lower()}"
    original_name = sanitize_filename(file.filename)

    content = await file.read()
    await s3_client.put_object(Bucket="uploads", Key=storage_key, Body=content)

    return {
        "storage_key": storage_key,
        "original_name": original_name,
        "content_type": file.content_type,
        "size": len(content)
    }
Files should not be stored on the API server's local filesystem in production — local storage is not shared across replicas and is lost on container restart. Stream directly to object storage:

import aioboto3
from fastapi import UploadFile, File
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager

S3_BUCKET = "my-app-uploads"

@asynccontextmanager
async def get_s3():
    session = aioboto3.Session()
    async with session.client(
        "s3",
        region_name="us-east-1",
        aws_access_key_id=os.environ["AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID"],
        aws_secret_access_key=os.environ["AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY"],
    ) as client:
        yield client

@app.post("/upload/")
async def upload_to_s3(file: UploadFile = File(...)):
    storage_key = f"uploads/{uuid.uuid4()}{Path(file.filename).suffix}"

    async with get_s3() as s3:
        await s3.upload_fileobj(
            file.file,           # Pass the underlying file-like object
            S3_BUCKET,
            storage_key,
            ExtraArgs={
                "ContentType": file.content_type,
                "ServerSideEncryption": "AES256",
            }
        )

    return {
        "key": storage_key,
        "url": f"https://{S3_BUCKET}.s3.amazonaws.com/{storage_key}"
    }
to S3 without buffering the entire content in Python memory.
For large files, skip the API server entirely by generating a pre-signed URL that allows the client to upload directly to S3:

@app.post("/uploads/presign/")
async def get_presigned_url(
    filename: str,
    content_type: str,
    current_user: User = Depends(get_current_user)
):
    storage_key = f"user-uploads/{current_user.id}/{uuid.uuid4()}"
    original_name = sanitize_filename(filename)

    async with get_s3() as s3:
        presigned_url = await s3.generate_presigned_url(
            "put_object",
            Params={
                "Bucket": S3_BUCKET,
                "Key": storage_key,
                "ContentType": content_type,
            },
            ExpiresIn=3600,     # URL valid for 1 hour
        )

    # Record pending upload in DB
    upload_id = await create_pending_upload_record(
        user_id=current_user.id,
        storage_key=storage_key,
        original_name=original_name
    )

    return {
        "upload_id": upload_id,
        "upload_url": presigned_url,
        "storage_key": storage_key,
        "expires_in": 3600
    }

@app.post("/uploads/{upload_id}/confirm/")
async def confirm_upload(
    upload_id: str,
    current_user: User = Depends(get_current_user)
):
    """Called by client after direct S3 upload completes."""
    await mark_upload_confirmed(upload_id, current_user.id)
    return {"status": "confirmed"}
This architecture eliminates the API server as a data path for file content entirely — it only handles metadata and authorization.

Client (Browser / Mobile / CLI)
         │
         │  POST /upload/
         │  Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=----WebKitFormBoundary...
         │
         │  ------WebKitFormBoundary...
         │  Content-Disposition: form-data; name="title"
         │
         │  My Document
         │  ------WebKitFormBoundary...
         │  Content-Disposition: form-data; name="file"; filename="report.pdf"
         │  Content-Type: application/pdf
         │
         │  <binary PDF content>
         │  ------WebKitFormBoundary...--
         ▼
[ Uvicorn / ASGI Server ]
         │   Receives raw HTTP stream
         ▼
[ python-multipart parser ]
         │   Parses boundary-delimited parts
         │   Creates SpooledTemporaryFile per part
         ▼
[ FastAPI Dependency Injection ]
         │   title: str = Form(...)   → extracted from text part
         │   file: UploadFile = File(...) → wrapped SpooledTemporaryFile
         ▼
[ Endpoint function ]
         │   await file.read()    → read from SpooledTemporaryFile
         │   Validate, transform, store
         ▼
[ Storage Layer ]
         │   Stream to S3 / local disk / database
         ▼
[ Response ]
where users upload files with metadata, the API validates content, stores to S3, extracts text for search, and generates thumbnails — all while maintaining memory efficiency:

from fastapi import FastAPI, UploadFile, File, Form, Depends, HTTPException, BackgroundTasks
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Optional, List
from pathlib import Path
import uuid, os, magic

app = FastAPI()

ALLOWED_TYPES = {
    "application/pdf": ".pdf",
    "image/jpeg": ".jpg",
    "image/png": ".png",
    "application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.wordprocessingml.document": ".docx",
    "text/plain": ".txt",
}
MAX_FILE_SIZE = 50 * 1024 * 1024   # 50MB
CHUNK_SIZE = 64 * 1024             # 64KB

class DocumentResponse(BaseModel):
    document_id: str
    filename: str
    content_type: str
    size_bytes: int
    storage_key: str
    processing_status: str

async def validate_and_buffer_upload(
    file: UploadFile,
    max_size: int = MAX_FILE_SIZE
) -> tuple[bytes, str]:
    """
    Stream file content, enforce size limit, detect true MIME type.
    Returns (content_bytes, detected_mime_type).
    """
    chunks = []
    total = 0

    while True:
        chunk = await file.read(CHUNK_SIZE)
        if not chunk:
            break
        total += len(chunk)
        if total > max_size:
            raise HTTPException(
                status_code=413,
                detail=f"File size exceeds {max_size // (1024 * 1024)}MB limit"
            )
        chunks.append(chunk)

    content = b"".join(chunks)

    # Detect actual MIME type from content, not client header
    detected_type = magic.from_buffer(content, mime=True)

    return content, detected_type

async def process_document_background(document_id: str, storage_key: str, content_type: str):
    """Background task: extract text and generate preview."""
    try:
        content = await download_from_s3(storage_key)
        if content_type == "application/pdf":
            text = await extract_pdf_text(content)
            await index_document_text(document_id, text)
            thumbnail = await generate_pdf_thumbnail(content)
            thumbnail_key = f"thumbnails/{document_id}.jpg"
            await upload_to_s3_bytes(thumbnail_key, thumbnail, "image/jpeg")
            await update_document_thumbnail(document_id, thumbnail_key)
        await update_document_status(document_id, "processed")
    except Exception as exc:
        import logging
        logging.error(f"Background processing failed for {document_id}: {exc}", exc_info=True)
        await update_document_status(document_id, "processing_failed")

@app.post(
    "/documents/",
    response_model=DocumentResponse,
    status_code=201
)
async def upload_document(
    background_tasks: BackgroundTasks,
    title: str = Form(..., min_length=1, max_length=255),
    description: Optional[str] = Form(default=None, max_length=2000),
    tags: str = Form(default=""),
    folder_id: Optional[str] = Form(default=None),
    file: UploadFile = File(...),
    current_user: User = Depends(get_current_user)
):
    # ── Filename sanitization ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
    safe_name = sanitize_filename(file.filename)
    if not safe_name:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Invalid filename")

    # ── Content validation ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    content, detected_type = await validate_and_buffer_upload(file)

    if detected_type not in ALLOWED_TYPES:
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=415,
            detail=f"File type '{detected_type}' not supported. "
                   f"Allowed: {', '.join(ALLOWED_TYPES.keys())}"
        )

    # ── Generate storage key with UUID (never use original filename as key) ──
    extension = ALLOWED_TYPES[detected_type]
    document_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    storage_key = f"documents/{current_user.id}/{document_id}{extension}"

    # ── Upload to S3 ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    async with get_s3() as s3:
        await s3.put_object(
            Bucket=S3_BUCKET,
            Key=storage_key,
            Body=content,
            ContentType=detected_type,
            ServerSideEncryption="AES256",
            Metadata={
                "original-filename": safe_name,
                "uploaded-by": current_user.id,
            }
        )

    # ── Save metadata to DB ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
    tag_list = [t.strip() for t in tags.split(",") if t.strip()]
    await save_document_record(
        document_id=document_id,
        user_id=current_user.id,
        title=title,
        description=description,
        original_filename=safe_name,
        storage_key=storage_key,
        content_type=detected_type,
        size_bytes=len(content),
        folder_id=folder_id,
        tags=tag_list,
        status="processing"
    )

    # ── Enqueue background processing ─────────────────────────────────────────
    background_tasks.add_task(
        process_document_background,
        document_id,
        storage_key,
        detected_type
    )

    return DocumentResponse(
        document_id=document_id,
        filename=safe_name,
        content_type=detected_type,
        size_bytes=len(content),
        storage_key=storage_key,
        processing_status="processing"
    )

@app.get("/documents/{document_id}/download/")
async def download_document(
    document_id: str,
    current_user: User = Depends(get_current_user)
):
    """Return a time-limited pre-signed download URL."""
    doc = await get_document_record(document_id)
    if not doc or doc.user_id != current_user.id:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Document not found")

    async with get_s3() as s3:
        download_url = await s3.generate_presigned_url(
            "get_object",
            Params={
                "Bucket": S3_BUCKET,
                "Key": doc.storage_key,
                "ResponseContentDisposition": f'attachment; filename="{doc.original_filename}"',
            },
            ExpiresIn=900   # 15 minutes
        )

    return {"download_url": download_url, "expires_in": 900}

# WRONG — loads 500MB video entirely into memory
async def upload_video(video: bytes = File(...)):
    ...
for files that may exceed a few hundred kilobytes.

size = len(content)
# ... later ...
content_again = await file.read()  # Returns b"" — cursor is at end of file
Always validate actual file content via magic bytes or a library like
4. Using original filename as storage path

# WRONG — path traversal attack: filename="../../etc/cron.d/malicious"
path = f"/uploads/{file.filename}"
Always sanitize filenames and prefer UUID-based storage keys.

If structured data must accompany a file, serialize it as a JSON string in a form field:

import json

async def create_post(
    metadata_json: str = Form(...),    # JSON string in form field
    image: UploadFile = File(...)
):
    metadata = PostMetadata.model_validate_json(metadata_json)
with JSON to create the record, then
7. Not closing or limiting concurrent upload memory

calls each hold their file content in memory simultaneously. With 100 concurrent 10MB uploads, this is 1GB of memory consumed instantaneously. Use streaming writes to disk or object storage as content arrives rather than buffering fully.
8. Not setting upload size limits in the reverse proxy

FastAPI/Uvicorn will accept any upload until Python-level code enforces a limit. A malicious client can hold a connection open while slowly sending a 100GB file. Enforce upload size limits at the nginx/proxy layer:
unless files are guaranteed tiny (< 100KB) and the volume of concurrent uploads is very low.
Stream large files to disk or object storage in chunks
— never buffer multi-megabyte content in Python memory.
— client-supplied MIME types cannot be trusted for security-sensitive operations.
and use UUID-based storage keys — never use client-supplied filenames directly in filesystem paths or object storage keys.
Set size limits at multiple layers
, Python-level chunk counting, and S3 multipart constraints.
for large file uploads — eliminates the API server as a bandwidth bottleneck and reduces compute cost.
Store file metadata separately from file content
— keep content type, original filename, size, upload user, and timestamp in the database; use the storage key as the content reference.
Add virus scanning for user-uploaded files
— integrate ClamAV or AWS GuardDuty Malware Protection for any application accepting files from untrusted users.
Generate thumbnails and extract text in background tasks
— these are CPU/IO-intensive operations that must not block the upload response.
Use separate S3 buckets or prefixes
for user uploads vs. application assets, and set appropriate bucket policies for each.
Serve downloads via pre-signed URLs with short TTLs
rather than proxying file content through the API server.
holds its full content in memory simultaneously. For an endpoint accepting 50MB files with 50 concurrent users, that is 2.5GB of peak memory. The solution is streaming:

# Memory usage: O(chunk_size) regardless of file size or concurrency
async with aiofiles.open(dest_path, "wb") as out:
    while chunk := await file.read(CHUNK_SIZE):
        await out.write(chunk)
S3 Multipart Upload for Very Large Files
For files above ~100MB, AWS recommends multipart uploads — they allow parallelism, support resumption, and avoid 5GB single-part limits:

import aioboto3

async def multipart_upload(file: UploadFile, bucket: str, key: str):
    session = aioboto3.Session()
    async with session.client("s3") as s3:
        # Initiate multipart upload
        response = await s3.create_multipart_upload(Bucket=bucket, Key=key)
        upload_id = response["UploadId"]

        parts = []
        part_number = 1
        min_part_size = 5 * 1024 * 1024  # 5MB minimum per S3 spec

        try:
            buffer = b""
            while True:
                chunk = await file.read(CHUNK_SIZE)
                if not chunk:
                    break
                buffer += chunk

                if len(buffer) >= min_part_size:
                    part = await s3.upload_part(
                        Bucket=bucket,
                        Key=key,
                        UploadId=upload_id,
                        PartNumber=part_number,
                        Body=buffer
                    )
                    parts.append({"PartNumber": part_number, "ETag": part["ETag"]})
                    part_number += 1
                    buffer = b""

            # Upload final (possibly smaller) part
            if buffer:
                part = await s3.upload_part(
                    Bucket=bucket, Key=key,
                    UploadId=upload_id, PartNumber=part_number, Body=buffer
                )
                parts.append({"PartNumber": part_number, "ETag": part["ETag"]})

            # Complete the upload
            await s3.complete_multipart_upload(
                Bucket=bucket, Key=key,
                UploadId=upload_id,
                MultipartUpload={"Parts": parts}
            )

        except Exception:
            # Abort incomplete upload to avoid S3 storage charges
            await s3.abort_multipart_upload(Bucket=bucket, Key=key, UploadId=upload_id)
            raise
To prevent upload storms from saturating memory or downstream storage bandwidth, use an asyncio semaphore:

Some files are valid in multiple formats simultaneously — a file can be a valid JPEG and a valid ZIP (and therefore a valid.docx) at the same time. Magic byte checking is necessary but not sufficient for files that will be executed or rendered. For full security, validate with domain-specific libraries:

from PIL import Image
import io

async def validate_image_safely(content: bytes) -> bool:
    """Use Pillow to fully decode the image — catches malformed or polyglot files."""
    try:
        img = Image.open(io.BytesIO(content))
        img.verify()   # Verifies without fully decoding
        return True
    except Exception:
        return False
Archive files (ZIP, TAR, GZIP) can decompress to sizes orders of magnitude larger than their compressed form. Never decompress untrusted archives without size limits:

Files uploaded by users and served back to other users should never be served from the same domain as the application — they may contain HTML, JavaScript, or SVG that executes in the browser with the application's origin:
Serve user uploads from a separate domain (e.g.,
to force download rather than browser rendering
Never render user-uploaded HTML or SVG files inline
must be installed explicitly — it is a required but non-bundled dependency for all form and file handling in FastAPI.
(JSON) cannot coexist in the same endpoint — use a JSON string in a form field or a two-step request pattern when structured data must accompany a file.
is almost always the correct choice over
that avoids loading entire files into memory and provides metadata like
is client-supplied and untrustworthy — always validate actual file content via magic bytes or a library like
Never use client-supplied filenames as filesystem paths or storage keys — sanitize aggressively and prefer UUID-based keys with the original name stored only in the database.
Stream large files to disk or object storage in chunks using
— buffering multi-megabyte content in Python memory multiplied across concurrent requests causes memory exhaustion.
Pre-signed S3 URLs are the production pattern for large file uploads — they eliminate the API server from the data path entirely, reducing cost and latency.
Enforce upload size limits at multiple layers: the reverse proxy (
), streaming chunk counting in Python, and storage system constraints.
User-uploaded files must never be served from the application's own domain — serve from a separate origin with
to prevent stored XSS via uploaded HTML or SVG content.
